Chapter 4 — Handoff Conditions: The Gate — Not "looks good." A specific, testable condition that must be true before the next step begins. This is what separates c

beats.text: This chapter gives a working overview of Handoff Conditions: The Gate Between Steps, focusing on the ideas a reader needs before moving to the next chapter. The chapter moves through Learning outcomes, Opening, What a handoff condition is, Strong vs. weak: the worked table, and related ideas. Read it for the main argument, the vocabulary it introduces, and the practical judgment it asks you to develop. Not "looks good." A specific, testable condition that must be true before the next step begins. This is what separates conducting from approving.

beats.text: The teacher reviewed Claude's output. The new About page rendered. The CSS looked right. The navigation included the new link. The teacher approved the commit and pushed. Three days later, a student emailed: the link to the syllabus from the About page does not work.

title: WHAT A HANDOFF
CONDITION IS.
segments.body_html: Specific.→Testable.→Binary.

beats.text: A handoff condition is the answer to one question: what must be true after this step before the next step begins? Three properties make a handoff condition load-bearing.

segments.label: 03 / 13 · WHAT A HANDOFF CONDITION IS

segments.section: STRONG VS. WEAK: THE WORKED TA
title: STRONG VS. WEAK:
THE WORKED TABLE
segments.body_html:
Add a new page — "looks good"; "renders"
Change CSS — "no errors"; "looks better"
Deploy to server — "rsync exits 0"

beats.text: For the class-website build's three main step types: Read the two columns. The weak column is what most teachers write when they write conditions at all. The strong column is what would have caught the syllabus-link bug from the opening.

segments.label: 04 / 13 · STRONG VS. WEAK: THE WORKED TA

segments.section: HOW TO WRITE ONE: FIVE QUESTIO
title: HOW TO WRITE ONE:
FIVE QUESTIONS
segments.body_html:
·Claim of step? Add an About page; do not modify other files.
·Artifact? The new src/about.html and one nav-link change in src/index.html.
·Verification? Run npm run lint-html against the new file and against the rendered live site after deploy.
·Threshold? Lint exits 0; every link 200s on the deployment.
·Failure signature? If a link 404s, the cause is probably a relative path that resolved on local dev and not on the schoo

beats.text: Five questions, asked in order, in the moment you would otherwise be approving the build step. What did the previous step claim to do? Read the specification you wrote in Chapter 3. The handoff condition is anchored to the operation, output format, and negative constraint elements.

segments.label: 05 / 13 · HOW TO WRITE ONE: FIVE QUESTIO

title: REVERT, DO NOT CORRECT FORWARD:
/REWIND
segments.body_html:
/rewind. Select the checkpoint before the failed step. Claude restores both the conversation context and the file changefrom both Claude's memory and your file system.
Respecify. Rewrite the original specification with the failure mode as an explicit negative constraint or as a new posit"All link paths in the new page must use absolute server-relative paths (leading /); never use page-relative paths that
·Re-run from clean state. Submit the revised specification. Claude executes against the corrected spec; the failure mode
·Re-verify. Run the handoff condition again. If it still fails, the specification is still wrong. /rewind again; tighten

beats.text: The hardest discipline when a handoff condition fails: do not write a follow-up prompt to fix the result. The instinct is to ask Claude to correct the failed step. "The link is broken; fix it." Claude composes a correction. Maybe it works. Maybe it produces a second wrong fix on top of the first wrong execution. Either way, the session's context now contains the failed step, the failure observation, the attempted correction, and Claude's reasoning across all of them.

segments.label: 06 / 13 · REVERT, DO NOT CORRECT FORWARD

title: SOME CONDITIONS REQUIRE
HUMAN REVIEW.
segments.body_html:
Not every handoff condition can be a command. Some require human judgment — "the table is laid out in a visually balance

Some conditions require human review — The discipline for non-automatable conditions: name which human, at what point, performs the review. For your own builds

beats.text: Not every handoff condition can be a command. Some require human judgment , "the table is laid out in a visually balanced way," "the tone of the feedback is appropriate for a tenth-grader." These are real handoff conditions; they cannot be automated. The discipline for non-automatable conditions: name which human, at what point, performs the review. For your own builds, that is you. For classroom work, that may be a peer reviewer.

segments.label: 07 / 13 · SOME CONDITIONS REQUIRE HUMAN

title: TEACHER BUILD:
HANDOFF CONDITIONS FOR YOUR CLASS-WEBSITE PAGES
segments.body_html:
·Before approving any build step, evaluate the handoff condition. If automated (lint, test, command), run it. If visual,
·If the condition fails, /rewind to before the step. Respecify with the failure as a negative constraint. Re-run.
·If you fail twice on the same step, /clear and rewrite the specification from scratch with both failures as constraints.

beats.text: Apply the chapter's discipline to your project. Pick three steps from your class-website build. For each, write a handoff condition in a one-sentence form that names the artifact, the verification method, and the threshold.

segments.label: 08 / 13 · TEACHER BUILD: HANDOFF CONDITI

title: CLASSROOM ACTIVITY:
STUDENTS DESIGN HANDOFF CONDITIONS
segments.body_html:
·Each condition is specific, testable, binary.
·At least one condition uses an automated check (a command, a lint, a test).
·At least one condition includes a human-review pause for a property that cannot be automated.

beats.text: Hand students a multi-step build sequence , extracting a CSV, processing the rows, writing a summary. Five steps. Each student writes a handoff condition for each step. Each condition is specific, testable, binary. At least one condition uses an automated check (a command, a lint, a test). At least one condition includes a human-review pause for a property that cannot be automated.

segments.label: 09 / 13 · CLASSROOM ACTIVITY: STUDENTS D

title: COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS
segments.body_html: "Looks good is a condition."→"Forward correction is fine."→"/rewind is for failures."→"Handoff conditions are for big builds."→"My grading scripts already work; I do not need handoff conditions."

beats.text: "Looks good is a condition." No. It is a feeling. Conditions are specific, testable, binary. "Forward correction is fine." Sometimes. Often it pollutes the context. The two-failure rule: after two failed corrections on the same step, /clear and start fresh with the failures as negative constraints.

segments.label: 10 / 13 · COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS

title: AI WAYBACK
MACHINE.
segments.body_html: W. Edwards Deming→Plan-Do-Check-Act→quality is built into a process through explicit verification at every step, not inspected in at the end→andon-cord

beats.text: W. Edwards Deming (1900, 1993) , American statistician whose Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle, articulated across Out of the Crisis (1986) and his decades of work with Japanese manufacturers, made the case that quality is built into a process through explicit verification at every step, not inspected in at the end. Deming's argument, against the dominant practice of his era, was that final inspection was the most expensive place to catch failure.

segments.label: 11 / 13 · AI WAYBACK MACHINE
